content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcnr- PRACTICEs CouuISSIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95811-2329
(9t6) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
September 19,2007
LeeAnn Williams
l00l-175 West Lambert Road
La Habra, Califomia 90631
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No.I-07-142
Dear Ms. Williams:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Because your questions are general in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the
reqllestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 l4;
Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
QUESTTON
Must a group that plans to address some recent school board decisions register as a
committee?
CONCLUSION
If the group receives contributions totaling $ 1,000 or more in a calendar year, or pools
funds totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of supporting or opposing
candidates or ballot measures (including a recall measure), the group will become a "recipient"
committee and will incur campaign disclosure reporting obligations pursuant to the Act.
I The Political Retbrm Act is contained in Covernment Code Sections 8 I 000 rhrough 9 t0l-1. All srarutory references
are to the Govcmment Code. unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair PoliticaL practices Commission are
contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califomia Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are ro
Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. l-07-142
Page No. 2
FACTS
Several citizens in your community are concerned with recent decisions made by your
local school board and you would like to vocalize your concerns as a group. You indicate that
the group will not support or oppose any candidates or ballot measures. However, the group will
address a possible recall of board members, address two recent decisions regarding school
district issues, and send a mailer and a telephone broadcast to inform citizens of the board's
recent decisions.
ANALYSIS
The Act imposes reporting requirements on candidates and "committees." A recipient
committee is any person or combination of persons who receives contributions totaling $1,000 or
more in a calendar year. (Section 82013(a).) In addition, when two or more individuals pool
money to make contributions or expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year, they are
required to file as a single recipient committee under Section 82013(a). (Lowell Advice Letter,
No. I-95-015; Culver Advice Letter, No. I-94-00; Mark, et al. Advice Letter, No. I-93-139: Gross
Advice Letter, No. A-93-128.)
The terms "contribution" and "expenditure" are generally defined as any monetary or
non-monetary payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is
not received. (Section 82015; Regulation 18215(a).) A payment is "made for political purposes"
if it is for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of
arry measure. (Regulations 18215(aXl); 18225(aX1).)
You indicate that the group will address a possible recall of school board members. The
Act defines "measure" to include a recall. (Section 82043.) A recall proposal becomes a
"measure" when proponents begin circulating petitions to qualify the proposal for the ballot. (/n
re Fontana (I976) 2 FPPC Ops. 25.) Once a proposal becomes a measure, all contributions
received and expenditures made must be reported, including those received and made before the
campaign reporting requirements were triggered. (Angus Advice Letter, No. A-97-173.)
An "independent expenditure" is a payment in connection with a communication that
expressly advocates the election or defeat of a candidate, the passage or defeat of a ballot
measure, or which unambiguously urges a particular result in an election. (Section 82031.) A
communication "expressly advocates" the nomination, election or defeat of a candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a measure if: (1) it contains express words of advocacy such as
"vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot," "vote against," "defeat," "reject," "sign
petitions for," or (2) it otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that the
communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election.
( Regul ation I 8225 (b)(2\.)


File No. l-07-142
Page No. 3
You state that the group plans to send a mailer and a telephone broadcast message to
inform citizens of some recent decisions made by the school board members. If the
communications that are sent to educate citizens about the school board's decisions do not
contain express advocacy, there are no disclosure requirements under the Act. However, if the
communications advocate either support for or opposition to a candidate or measure (including a
recall petition), then payments made for the costs of that communication will be reportable if the
expenditures total $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
Therefore, if the group receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year
or pools money to make expenditures of $1,000 or more in a calendar year, the group will qualify
as a recipient committee under the Act. The first filing required for a recipient committee is the
Statement of Organization (Form 410), which is due within 10 days of reciiving contributions
totaling $1,000. (Section 84101.) If the contributions received or expenditures made are for the
purpose of qualifying or supporting a potential recall, the committee will be required to file
periodic campaign reports as a primarily formed ballot measure committee. (Section g2047.5.)
The filing requirements for primarily formed ballot measure committees are explained in detail in
the FPPC Campaign Information Manual 3, which is available on our website.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Political Reform Counsultant
Technical Assistance Division
TLS:jgl

committee?

CONCLUSION: If the group receives contributions totaling $ 1,000 or more in a calendar year, or pools
funds totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year for the purpose of supporting or opposing
candidates or ballot measures (including a recall measure), the group will become a "recipient"
committee and will incur campaign disclosure reporting obligations pursuant to the Act.
